topic: CAT-005 Refraction Cascade — Revision 2 addressing 7 curmudgeon blockers
gate_verdict_detail: All 7 blockers from Cycle 1 are addressed. The revision is substantially more conservative and more rigorous. One remaining disagreement: WIN-018 flip should be rejected. Everything else passes gate 2.

B1_per_win_audit.detail: Analyst re-read all 7 flagged WINs against actual wins.json content. WIN-010 reversal to STAY PUT is a good correction — C1 was arguably too generous in accepting that flip. The corrections are honest and well-reasoned. However, WIN-018 flip should still be rejected (see per-WIN assessment below).

B2_refuted_by_data_protection.detail: WIN-017 and WIN-026 stay at Refuted by Data. No downgrades. Correct.

B3_dual_label_withdrawn.detail: WIN-016 dual-label withdrawn cleanly. Verdict stays Refuted by Data.

B4_numerics_verification.detail: EXP-050-numerics.json independently verified. All key claims check out: refraction angle range 6.3-29.0 deg (verified via arctan computation), angular diameter modulation 83.1% (verified: (3617-1976)/1976), tidal ratio 6.14x (verified: (3617/1976)^3). The correction of the 400-675x claim was important — the original conflated zenith and horizon comparisons. The corrected 11-51x vs horizon / 210-967x vs zenith framing is more accurate. The n(r) profile correction (n=1.235 at sun orbit radius, not producing 0.04 deg starlight bending) actually STRENGTHENS the argument. Numerics companion file is now a solid foundation for prose.

B5_cascade_dependent_tag_dropped.detail: Tag dropped per C1 direction. Cascade-affected WINs identified by prose cross-references instead. Clean solution.

B6_structural_title.detail: '1.8 The Refraction Cascade: When One Fix Breaks Everything' is version-independent and descriptive. Good.

B7_section_number.detail: Section 1.8 avoids collision with existing Section 1.7 (The Gravity Paradox). Verified.

WIN-010.r2_proposal: STAY PUT at Std Model Explains
WIN-010.rationale: R2 is correct that WIN-010 is about eclipse magnetic MAGNITUDE (Chapman mechanism), not shadow POSITION. The cascade Point 6 (eclipse geometry paradox) concerns where the shadow falls, not how the ionospheric conductivity changes. Chapman's mechanism operates regardless of precise sun altitude because it depends on UV flux reduction during totality, not on geometric altitude. C1 was too generous in accepting this flip. R2's correction is sound.

WIN-016.c1_verdict: Reject flip — Refuted by Data + cascade supplementary
WIN-016.r2_proposal: STAY PUT at Refuted by Data + supplementary note
WIN-016.rationale: No change needed. Gaia kill stands.

WIN-017.c1_verdict: Reject flip — Refuted by Data + cascade supplementary
WIN-017.r2_proposal: STAY PUT at Refuted by Data + supplementary note
WIN-017.rationale: No change needed. 1.8B star parallax kill stands.

WIN-018.rationale: The analyst's argument is that the 83% physical altitude variation would modulate the equation of time. This conflates two orthogonal quantities. The equation of time depends on the sun's angular speed variation (determined by disc orbital radius, which varies from r=7401 km at Cancer to r=12614 km at Capricorn — a designed feature of the dome model) and the obliquity projection effect. The cascade changes the sun's ALTITUDE at each radius, not the disc radius itself. In the dome model, the refraction n(r) is radially symmetric — it bends light rays radially (changing apparent altitude) but cannot change the sun's apparent azimuthal position (right ascension). The equation of time is an RA phenomenon. The physical altitude variation (1975-3618 km) is compensated by n(r) to produce the observed apparent altitude (5733 km), and this compensation doesn't affect the RA at all. A dome defender would correctly point out: 'The apparent solar track is what determines the analemma. The refraction preserves the apparent position. The physical altitude is irrelevant to the observed day-length variation.' This defense holds because the equation of time doesn't depend on altitude. The existing Misleading verdict (cherry-picked sub-statistic with no dome derivation) is the correct and more defensible framing.
WIN-018.other_side: The analyst could argue that the physical orbit being at drastically different altitudes creates timing effects through path-length differences in the refractive medium. This is a second-order effect that might produce microsecond-level timing anomalies, not the 6.9-minute signal the WIN claims. The argument is too speculative for a verdict flip.

WIN-019.rationale: R2 correctly recognizes the 2.66 loop ratio is altitude-independent.

WIN-026.c1_verdict: Reject flip — keep Refuted by Data
WIN-026.r2_proposal: STAY PUT at Refuted by Data
WIN-026.rationale: R2's analysis is particularly insightful: 'the cascade makes the dome argument slightly BETTER for crepuscular rays by keeping the apparent altitude fixed.' This is a mature assessment.

WIN-044.gate2_verdict: AGREE — both C1 and R2 concur
WIN-044.rationale: H(r)-derived predictions inherit the cascade indeterminacy. Clean structural self-contradiction.

WIN-056.c1_verdict: Accept — add cascade citation, increase prominence
WIN-056.rationale: Anchor WIN for the cascade. Elevated prominence is editorially sound.

WIN-068.gate2_verdict: AGREE — both C1 and R2 concur
WIN-068.rationale: Eclipse geometry paradox (dome can't specify consistent physical sun altitude during eclipses) is structurally stronger than 'standard physics explains it better' because it doesn't require the reader to accept standard physics. Condition from C1 remains: retain Chapman rebuttal as secondary evidence.
WIN-068.condition: Chapman 1933 ionospheric mechanism must be retained as secondary evidence in the detail panel

revised_final_flip_table.method: Gate 2 curmudgeon adjudication of R2 flip table

section_proposal_assessment.word_budget: 400 words net-new (per C1). R2 acknowledges this.
section_proposal_assessment.key_instruction: Section 1.8 prose must open with a one-sentence cross-reference to Section 1.5, NOT restate the collision analysis. The cascade's contribution is the seasonal dimension (13-27 deg vs Section 1.5's 6-9 deg ceiling case). The n(r) inconsistency (n=1.235 at sun orbit, catastrophic for stellar astrometry) is the single strongest technical claim and should get the most space.
section_proposal_assessment.table_rows: Should be 8 rows (2 flips + 3 citations + 3 supplementary), not 12.

holes_found.description: WIN-018 flip proposal persists despite C1 rejection. The analyst's R2 argument about 'effective solar orbit radius' conflates disc orbital radius (horizontal, determines equation of time) with physical altitude (vertical, irrelevant to RA). The cascade changes altitude, not disc radius. The equation of time is an RA phenomenon. This is the same apparent-position defense from C1, which R2 did not adequately rebut.
holes_found.recommendation: Remove WIN-018 from flip table. Keep at Misleading. The cascade argument for analemma WINs requires a rigorous analysis of how n(r) interacts with the full equation of time — which has not been done. Without that analysis, the flip is speculative.

holes_found.description: R2 uses 'effective solar orbit radius' loosely in WIN-018 rationale, which could mislead the prose writer into making the same conflation. The physical orbit radius (on the disc) and the physical altitude are different quantities in the dome model.
holes_found.recommendation: If this phrasing appears in the prose, flag it. The cascade section must clearly distinguish disc-plane radius (r) from altitude (H(r)).

holes_found.description: R2 correctly notes the 0.04 deg starlight bending claim was wrong but the replacement argument ('multi-degree bending') is qualitative. A proper ray-trace has not been done and the numerics file acknowledges this (ray_trace_needed: true). The prose should be careful to state the qualitative impossibility without claiming a specific starlight bending angle.
holes_found.recommendation: Section 1.8 prose should frame the n(r) inconsistency as: 'n(r) at the sun's orbital radius (1.235) is catastrophically inconsistent with Gaia microarcsecond stellar astrometry' rather than citing a specific starlight bending angle. The qualitative argument is airtight; a specific number without a ray-trace is not.

stronger_arguments.description: R2's insight that the cascade makes the dome argument BETTER for crepuscular rays (WIN-026) is analytically sophisticated and should be referenced in the prose as an example of good-faith engagement. Acknowledging where the cascade does NOT apply strengthens credibility for the WINs where it does.

The physical-vs-apparent position classification that both C1 and R2 converge on is itself a powerful analytical framework. The prose should explicitly name these two classes: 'physical-position predictions' (eclipses, angular diameter, tidal forces, H(r)-derived) vs 'apparent-position observations' (analemma, crepuscular rays, sky coordinates). The cascade only breaks the former. Naming the distinction pre-empts the dome defender's strongest counter.

kernel_of_truth.description: The R2 revision demonstrates genuine analytical maturity. The analyst read the C1 blockers carefully, re-examined every flagged WIN against the actual data, corrected two overclaims (WIN-010, the 400-675x ratio), preserved the strong arguments, and produced independently verifiable numerics. The revision is substantially more conservative than v1 (from 10 proposed flips to 3, which I further trim to 2). This is exactly the trajectory a good revision should follow.
kernel_of_truth.why_one_modification_remains: WIN-018 persists because the analyst correctly identifies that the equation of time depends on the solar orbit, but incorrectly concludes that the cascade altitude variation affects the equation of time. This is a subtle error: the equation of time depends on RA (azimuthal), and the cascade affects altitude (radial). A radially symmetric n(r) cannot change apparent RA. The analyst may be right that there are second-order effects, but 'moderate confidence' is not sufficient for a verdict flip that the dome defender can directly rebut.

modifications_required: Remove WIN-018 from flip table — keep at Misleading. The cascade altitude variation does not reach the equation of time because n(r) is radially symmetric and cannot change apparent RA.
Final flip table: 2 flips (WIN-044 strong, WIN-068 moderate), 3 citation-adds (WIN-046, WIN-056, WIN-067), 3 supplementary (WIN-016, WIN-017, WIN-065).
Prose must distinguish physical-position predictions from apparent-position observations explicitly.
n(r) inconsistency claim must remain qualitative (no specific starlight bending angle) until a proper ray-trace is done.
Retain Chapman rebuttal as secondary evidence for WIN-068.
prose_writing_conditions: Physical vs apparent position distinction explicit in prose
Chapman retained as secondary for WIN-068
